einstein_strengths: The cognitive science vs. information retrieval reframing is the analysis's most valuable insight — it changes the optimization target from 'retention fidelity' to 'retrieval precision', which is measurable and tunable
Each of the four simplification proposals includes concrete implementation sketches, feasibility ratings (all high), and honest tradeoff analysis
The consistency boundary analysis is rigorous: three storage engines create 27-125 potential failure modes, treated as individual sharp edges rather than symptoms of excessive surface area
Six assumptions surfaced with specific validation methods — not just 'this might be wrong' but 'here is how to test whether it is wrong'
The handoff notes to Beethoven are precise and actionable, identifying exactly where theoretical and practical perspectives might diverge
analysis_perspectives.einstein_summary: Agent memory is fundamentally an information retrieval problem, not a cognitive science problem. The design borrows human memory metaphors (FSRS, Ebbinghaus, STM/LTM, sleep consolidation) that optimize for the wrong objective. Four concrete simplifications proposed: (1) replace FSRS/Ebbinghaus with BM25 + vector similarity + access-frequency, (2) eliminate bbolt in favor of sync.Map, (3) make Obsidian vault write-only from agent with batch import on startup, (4) defer all graph algorithms to consolidation phase. The Obsidian vault is identified as the genuine architectural differentiator.
staff_architect_strengths: Caught the AGPL-3 license contradiction that would be a distribution poison pill — this is a showstopper that the design documents missed entirely
Identified three concrete deployment blockers (license, agents-index.json, file location) with exact remediation steps and effort estimates
The convention separation recommendation (M-2) correctly identifies the go-tui pattern as the template: agent identity in .md, framework patterns in convention file
Failure mode analysis is practical and specific: 'agent deployed without agents-index.json entry' has high probability AND high impact — the most dangerous failure mode is the most likely one
Seven commendations balance the critique, correctly identifying the sharp edges file as the most thorough in the ecosystem and the CORRECT/WRONG pattern as well-executed

assumptions_to_validate.assumption: Agent memory access patterns are fundamentally IR (search-based), not cognitive (recall-based)
assumptions_to_validate.validation_method: Instrument existing memory system for 30 days. Log which memories are in context when agents produce correct/helpful responses. Compare distribution against both FSRS predictions and BM25 rankings. If BM25 ranking correlates more strongly with 'useful memory' than FSRS decay, Einstein's reframing is empirically confirmed.

assumptions_to_validate.assumption: 10K-node graph is the realistic scale for 1-2 years
assumptions_to_validate.validation_method: Run the proposed extraction pipeline on 10 real sessions. Count entities + relationships. Multiply by session frequency to project growth rate. If >100 entities/session, the 10K ceiling is reached in ~100 sessions (2-3 months), fundamentally changing the chromem-go viability calculus.

assumptions_to_validate.assumption: Sub-100ms total retrieval is achievable within the existing hook chain budget
assumptions_to_validate.validation_method: Profile gogent-load-context, gogent-validate, and gogent-sharp-edge to measure p50/p95 execution times. Subtract from 100ms to determine the actual retrieval budget available for the knowledge graph.

assumptions_to_validate.assumption: chromem-go performs adequately on agent memory content types (code snippets, error messages, architectural decisions)
assumptions_to_validate.validation_method: Embed 100 representative agent memories using Ollama's nomic-embed-text. Run 20 retrieval queries. Evaluate whether semantic similarity correctly identifies relevant memories for code-heavy and error-heavy content.

assumptions_to_validate.assumption: Convention separation (agent.md + db-conventions.md) reduces per-invocation context cost meaningfully
assumptions_to_validate.validation_method: After factoring, measure token count of agent identity alone vs agent + convention. Verify convention is only loaded when auto_activate paths match, not on every invocation.

assumptions_to_validate.assumption: Graph structure adds sufficient value over pure vector similarity to justify the schema complexity
assumptions_to_validate.validation_method: Manually construct 20 representative queries agents would ask of their memory. Classify each as 'answerable by vector similarity alone' vs 'requires graph structure.' If >80% are vector-answerable, consider deferring graph schema entirely and starting with pure vector store + metadata filtering.

convergence_points.einstein_view: The Obsidian vault provides human-agent knowledge sharing that no other component offers. It should be preserved but simplified to write-only from agent perspective.
convergence_points.staff_architect_view: The fsnotify sharp edges demonstrate real deployment awareness. The vault sync layer is complex but the agent's documentation of its pitfalls (duplicate events, no recursive watch, write feedback loop) shows mature engineering.
convergence_points.synthesis: Both perspectives validate the vault layer's value. Einstein's simplification (write-only v1) actually strengthens Staff-Architect's position by eliminating the three most complex failure modes (fsnotify, debouncing, feedback loops) while preserving the human browsability that makes it valuable. The sharp edges for vault sync should be retained as documentation for a future v2 bidirectional implementation.
convergence_points.topic: Obsidian vault is the genuine architectural differentiator

convergence_points.einstein_view: Not directly addressed (different scope), but the four simplifications would naturally reduce implementation detail by eliminating bbolt, simplifying vault sync, and removing runtime graph traversal code.
convergence_points.staff_architect_view: At 882 lines (2.4x go-pro), the agent is an 'implementation cookbook, not agent identity.' Recommends factoring into go-db-architect.md (~400 lines) + db-conventions.md (~400 lines).
convergence_points.synthesis: After adopting Einstein's simplifications, the agent definition shrinks naturally: bbolt patterns (~80 lines), runtime traversal code (~60 lines), and bidirectional sync patterns (~50 lines) become irrelevant. The convention separation is still needed for the remaining SQL schema and algorithm implementations, but the delta is smaller. Target: ~350 lines agent identity + ~300 lines db-conventions.md.
convergence_points.topic: Agent definition is too implementation-heavy for its role

convergence_points.einstein_view: The no-CGO constraint is identified as a hard technical constraint. The AGPL issue is implicit in the fundamental constraints analysis (single-binary distribution + no GPL).
convergence_points.staff_architect_view: Three explicit blockers: C-1 (AGPL license contradiction), M-1 (no agents-index.json entry), M-3 (wrong file location). Agent is 'inert' without these resolved.
convergence_points.synthesis: These are unambiguous prerequisites. The AGPL issue is the most consequential: Staff-Architect's recommendation to remove coder/hnsw and cap at chromem-go's 100K limit is correct for v1, especially since Einstein's analysis suggests 10K nodes is the realistic near-term scale. The agents-index.json entry and file relocation are mechanical but blocking.
convergence_points.topic: Three deployment blockers must be resolved before implementation

convergence_points.einstein_view: Theoretically unjustified: 5ms→0.5ms improvement is imperceptible, SQLite's page cache already provides hot reads, bbolt adds a full consistency boundary with 5+ failure modes. Recommends sync.Map replacement.
convergence_points.staff_architect_view: Doesn't directly challenge bbolt's inclusion but notes missing test patterns for it (m-4) and documents the byte-slice-lifetime sharp edge as exactly the kind of issue caught by tests. The assumption register (A-2) marks sub-100ms retrieval as unverified.
convergence_points.synthesis: Einstein's theoretical case for removing bbolt is strong and uncontested by Staff-Architect's practical review. Staff-Architect's identification of missing bbolt test patterns further supports removal — if a component is hard to test and provides imperceptible benefit, it shouldn't exist. Adopt Einstein's sync.Map proposal.
convergence_points.topic: bbolt cache layer provides insufficient benefit for its complexity cost

convergence_points.einstein_view: Existing memory-archivist uses flat retrieval (grep, tag filter, glob). Runtime agent queries don't require multi-hop traversal. Graph algorithms should be consolidation-only, with pre-computed features served as column reads at runtime.
convergence_points.staff_architect_view: Notes that sub-100ms retrieval with recursive CTEs at 10K nodes is unverified (A-2). The gonum fallback for >10K edges is documented but untested. The overall design is 'implementation-cookbook' heavy on traversal code.
convergence_points.synthesis: Both analyses agree traversal is secondary to flat retrieval for runtime. Einstein provides the theoretical justification (agent query patterns are search-based), Staff-Architect provides the practical concern (performance unverified). Pre-computing graph features during consolidation satisfies both: Einstein's cleaner runtime path AND Staff-Architect's latency budget concerns.
convergence_points.topic: Graph traversal is over-invested for runtime use cases

divergence_resolution.einstein_position: Questionable validity. For most agent memories, valid_time and tx_time are identical. Only retroactive corrections need it, and those can be handled by a simpler 'supersedes' pointer. Marginal preference to keep because retrofitting is expensive and the cost (4 columns) is low, but demands concrete examples of bi-temporal queries agents would actually execute.
divergence_resolution.reasoning: The cost asymmetry resolves this: adding 4 columns costs minutes, retrofitting bi-temporal later costs days. The risk is paying minutes for something potentially unused (low cost) vs. paying days to retrofit something that turns out to be needed (high cost). Expected value favors keeping the columns.
divergence_resolution.resolution: Keep bi-temporal modeling but defer validation. Einstein is right that no concrete bi-temporal query examples exist in the design, and Staff-Architect is right that the implementation cost is low. The resolution is to implement the 4 extra columns (cheap) but require concrete query examples before building bi-temporal query utilities (expensive). If after 3 months of operation no bi-temporal query has been articulated, the columns are inert schema and can be ignored. This satisfies Einstein's 'prove it's needed' requirement while preserving Staff-Architect's 'retrofitting is expensive' concern.
divergence_resolution.staff_architect_position: Commends it as 'architecturally sophisticated' — provides both 'what was true when' and 'what did the system believe when' queries, critical for a memory system handling corrections.
divergence_resolution.topic: Bi-temporal modeling: necessary sophistication or YAGNI?

divergence_resolution.einstein_position: Not directly addressed, but the consistency boundary analysis suggests many sharp edges are symptoms of excessive architectural surface area rather than independent pitfalls. If bbolt is removed, ~5 sharp edges become irrelevant. If vault sync is simplified, ~3 more disappear.
divergence_resolution.reasoning: The quantity debate is resolved by recognizing that sharp edges count should be proportional to genuine complexity, not to an arbitrary ecosystem average. The 5-8 norm reflects simpler agents (CLI tools, TUI components). A database architecture agent legitimately has more pitfalls. But Einstein's simplifications do make some edges irrelevant, so the file should reflect the actual architecture being built.
divergence_resolution.resolution: The 25 sharp edges reflect the full pre-simplification architecture. After adopting Einstein's simplifications, the relevant count drops to approximately 15-17: ~5 bbolt edges become irrelevant, ~3 vault sync edges become v2-only documentation. The remaining 15-17 is still above the 5-8 ecosystem norm, but this is justified by the domain's genuine complexity (SQLite + vector search + graph schema + Markdown generation). The sharp edges file should be version-tagged: 'v1 (simplified)' edges active, 'v2 (full)' edges retained as documentation.
divergence_resolution.staff_architect_position: Commends the 25 sharp edges as 'the most thorough sharp edges file in the ecosystem' with real-world references. But handoff notes ask whether 25 represents over-specification vs the 5-8 ecosystem norm.
divergence_resolution.topic: Sharp edges quantity: thoroughness vs over-specification

divergence_resolution.einstein_position: Fundamental reframing needed. FSRS/Ebbinghaus are theoretically inapplicable to agents. Replace with BM25 + vector similarity + access-frequency boosting. The design borrows human memory metaphors without interrogating whether they apply.
divergence_resolution.reasoning: Einstein's theoretical case is airtight: agents don't forget, retrieval doesn't strengthen traces, there is no spacing effect. Staff-Architect's silence on this topic means there's no practical counter-argument. However, discarding the metaphor entirely loses communication value — users understand 'this memory is fading' better than 'this document's BM25 relevance score is declining.' Separation of concerns: IR for the engine, cognitive metaphor for the UI.
divergence_resolution.resolution: Adopt Einstein's reframing for the implementation while preserving the cognitive science language as a communication tool. Concretely: (1) the scoring function should use BM25 + vector similarity + access-frequency + time-decay, not FSRS/Ebbinghaus curves, (2) the system_design.md should be updated to use IR terminology (relevance scoring, not forgetting curves), but (3) the user-facing Obsidian vault can still use terms like 'memory strength' and 'consolidation' because these are intuitive for humans browsing their knowledge base. The math changes; the metaphor persists only at the UI layer.
divergence_resolution.staff_architect_position: Does not evaluate the theoretical framing (out of scope for practical review). Accepts the FSRS scoring as presented and reviews the implementation patterns around it.
divergence_resolution.topic: Cognitive science framing: keep the metaphor or replace with IR?

divergence_resolution.einstein_position: Implicitly suggests less implementation detail is needed after simplifications. Four major subsystems (bbolt, bidirectional sync, runtime traversal, FSRS scoring) would be removed or simplified.
divergence_resolution.reasoning: Staff-Architect's handoff notes ask whether for 'highly domain-specific agents where the domain knowledge IS the value,' the implementation-cookbook approach might actually be superior. The answer is: the knowledge IS valuable, but its location is wrong. Convention files are conditionally loaded and don't inflate every invocation's context. Agent identity files are always loaded. Putting implementation detail in the convention layer preserves the knowledge while respecting context budgets.
divergence_resolution.resolution: Staff-Architect's convention separation recommendation is correct AND is reinforced by Einstein's simplifications. The implementation detail is currently a bug (wrong location, inflates context) but the detail itself is valuable — it should move to db-conventions.md, not be deleted. After Einstein's simplifications, the convention file shrinks from ~400 to ~300 lines (removing bbolt patterns, simplifying vault sync patterns). The agent identity file should contain: role, constraints, approved dependencies, focus area rules (CORRECT/WRONG only), testing strategy, output requirements. Estimated: ~350 lines.
divergence_resolution.staff_architect_position: Explicitly identifies it as a 'major' issue (M-2). The 882-line agent is 2.4x peer average. Complete function implementations belong in a convention file, not the agent identity. The go-tui pattern (agent.md + bubbletea-convention.md) is the correct template.
divergence_resolution.topic: Whether go-db-architect's implementation detail is a feature or a bug

executive_summary: The embedded temporal knowledge graph is architecturally sound in its core concept — structured knowledge with vector search for agent memory — but is over-engineered in four dimensions that Einstein correctly identifies: cognitive science framing (should be IR), bbolt cache layer (should be sync.Map), bidirectional vault sync (should be write-only for v1), and runtime graph traversal (should be consolidation-only). The go-db-architect subagent definition is the most thoroughly researched agent in the ecosystem (25 sharp edges, 11 focus areas) but has three blocking deployment issues (AGPL license contradiction, missing agents-index.json entry, wrong file location) and encodes the full pre-simplification architecture — meaning the agent definition must be updated after the architectural simplifications are adopted. Both tracks receive CONDITIONAL GO: proceed with implementation only after adopting Einstein's four simplifications and resolving Staff-Architect's three blockers.

open_questions.question: What is the actual entity extraction rate per session, and does it support or undermine the 10K-node scale assumption?
open_questions.suggested_next_step: Run extraction pipeline on 10 real GOgent-Fortress sessions. Count entities + relationships produced. Multiply by session frequency to project graph growth rate.

open_questions.question: How much of the existing hook chain's 100ms latency budget remains for memory retrieval?
open_questions.suggested_next_step: Profile gogent-load-context, gogent-validate, and gogent-sharp-edge to measure p50/p95 execution times. This determines whether the <50ms retrieval target in the simplified architecture is achievable.

open_questions.question: What percentage of useful memory retrievals would benefit from graph traversal vs simple semantic/keyword search?
open_questions.suggested_next_step: Analyze 30 days of memory-archivist query patterns. Classify each as flat-retrieval or would-benefit-from-traversal. This provides empirical grounding for whether the graph schema investment is justified at all.

open_questions.question: Should go-db-architect be added to memory-archivist's 'can_spawn' list as a subordinate, or should they be peers routed independently?
open_questions.suggested_next_step: Map the workflow: does memory-archivist need to invoke go-db-architect for storage operations, or do they serve completely different trigger patterns? If the knowledge graph replaces memory-archivist's storage backend, go-db-architect is a dependency, not a peer.

open_questions.question: Does the 'pre-compaction flush' pattern produce useful memories or mostly noise?
open_questions.suggested_next_step: Implement the flush as a standalone experiment across 20 sessions. If most flushes produce NO_REPLY (as OpenClaw reports suggest), the feature is noise and should be deprioritized.

open_questions.question: Are there concrete bi-temporal queries that agents would actually execute?
open_questions.suggested_next_step: Before Phase 3 implementation, articulate at least 3 bi-temporal queries with realistic scenarios. If none can be articulated, keep the columns but don't build query utilities around them.

problem_statement.clarified: Dual-track evaluation requiring synthesis: Track 1 assesses whether the theoretical architecture is sound for agent memory (not human memory), while Track 2 assesses whether the practical agent definition is deployable within GOgent-Fortress. The tracks are coupled: architectural simplifications from Track 1 directly impact the content and size of the Track 2 agent definition.
problem_statement.original: Evaluate the embedded temporal knowledge graph plan (SQLite + bbolt + chromem-go, FSRS decay, three-phase loading, Obsidian vault) and the go-db-architect subagent definition for stylistic alignment, convention separation, sharp edges completeness, and /implement workflow fit.
success_criteria: GO/NO-GO verdict on the knowledge graph architecture with specific simplification requirements
GO/NO-GO verdict on go-db-architect fitness with prioritized deployment blockers
Specific recommendations for convention separation (agent identity vs db-conventions.md)
Assessment of sharp edges coverage relative to both proposed and simplified architectures
Integration roadmap showing how simplified architecture extends memory-archivist

risk_assessment.mitigation: Validate extraction precision on 50 representative sessions before committing to auto-extraction. If precision < 0.8, add human-in-the-loop review or restrict to high-confidence extractions only.
risk_assessment.risk: Entity extraction produces noisy graphs that degrade retrieval quality faster than value accumulates

risk_assessment.mitigation: Benchmark at 100K vectors before committing (Staff-Architect A-3). If degradation occurs, implement pruning strategy or the custom HNSW fallback. Einstein's entity extraction rate analysis should inform growth projections.
risk_assessment.risk: chromem-go's exhaustive search degrades beyond 50K vectors, reaching the ceiling before anticipated

risk_assessment.mitigation: Profile gogent-load-context, gogent-validate, and gogent-sharp-edge p50/p95 before implementation. If >50ms consumed, either optimize existing hooks or move retrieval to async injection after first response.
risk_assessment.risk: Existing hook chain latency budget leaves insufficient room for knowledge graph retrieval

risk_assessment.mitigation: Configure auto_activate paths (internal/memory/**, internal/graphstore/**) in agents-index.json. Path-based activation takes priority over trigger matching. Monitor routing logs for first 10 invocations.
risk_assessment.risk: Trigger collision between go-db-architect and go-pro on 'memory' and 'implement' keywords causes misrouting

risk_assessment.mitigation: Design storage interfaces that accommodate future bbolt layer and bidirectional sync without breaking changes. Staff-Architect's recommendation to define interfaces first (m-5) directly supports this.
risk_assessment.risk: Simplified architecture (no bbolt, write-only vault) proves insufficient and requires costly retrofitting

risk_assessment.mitigation: Create a test vault with 50 representative files, parse and render, diff against originals. Any data loss is a user trust issue that must be resolved before vault export goes live.
risk_assessment.risk: goldmark + abhinav extension stack has lossy round-trip for Obsidian-specific syntax (highlights, callouts, complex wikilinks)

not_recommended.approach: Implementing the full pre-simplification architecture (three storage engines, FSRS scoring, bidirectional vault sync, runtime graph traversal) as described in the original design documents
not_recommended.reason: Einstein's analysis demonstrates this optimizes for the wrong objective (cognitive retention vs retrieval precision), multiplies consistency failure modes (27-125 potential issues across three engines), and invests heavily in runtime capabilities (graph traversal) that serve <10% of actual query patterns. The simplified architecture delivers 90%+ of the value at ~40% of the complexity.

not_recommended.approach: Deploying go-db-architect without resolving the AGPL license issue
not_recommended.reason: Staff-Architect correctly identifies this as a distribution poison pill. AGPL-3 on coder/hnsw could require open-sourcing the entire binary. The 100K vector cap with chromem-go (MIT) is sufficient for the 10K-node near-term scale both analysts agree on.

not_recommended.approach: Skipping the convention separation and deploying the 882-line agent definition as-is
not_recommended.reason: 882 lines of agent identity inflates every invocation's context by ~18K tokens (9% of 200K window). Convention files are loaded conditionally. The go-tui pattern (agent + convention) is proven and reduces per-invocation cost while preserving all domain knowledge.

decision_points: Confirm chromem-go's MIT dual-license is acceptable (should be straightforward)
Decide thinking_budget for agents-index.json entry (recommend 14000, matching the go-db-architect frontmatter)
Decide which impl-manager.can_spawn and orchestrator.can_spawn lists to add go-db-architect to
implementation_phases.description: Resolve deployment blockers and update agent definition. Remove coder/hnsw from approved dependencies (cap at chromem-go 100K). Create agents-index.json entry. Move files to ~/.claude/agents/go-db-architect/. Factor into agent identity + db-conventions.md. Update scoring section to use IR-based approach instead of FSRS/Ebbinghaus.
success_criteria: go-db-architect appears in agents-index.json with all required fields
gogent-validate routes to go-db-architect for 'graph schema' and 'knowledge graph' triggers
Identity injection loads go-db-architect.md from ~/.claude/agents/go-db-architect/
Agent identity is <=400 lines; db-conventions.md is <=350 lines
No AGPL dependencies in approved list

decision_points: Validate chromem-go performance with agent memory content types (code, errors, decisions)
Choose BM25 implementation: SQLite FTS5 rank function vs Go-side scoring
Set initial weights for composite scoring (alpha, beta, gamma, delta) — will be tuned later
implementation_phases.description: Implement core storage layer: SQLite schema (nodes, edges, bi-temporal columns), sync.Map cache for core blocks, basic CRUD operations. Implement IR-based scoring (BM25 + vector similarity + recency).
success_criteria: SQLite schema created with nodes, edges, bi-temporal columns, FTS5 index
sync.Map cache loads core blocks in <5ms
End-to-end retrieval (query → ranked results) in <50ms at 1K nodes
All tests pass including edge cases from sharp edges

decision_points: goldmark + abhinav extension stack: validate round-trip fidelity with Obsidian test vault
Consolidation trigger: session-end only vs periodic (recommend session-end for v1)
Entity extraction: validate precision on representative sessions before committing to auto-extraction
implementation_phases.description: Implement Obsidian vault export (write-only) and batch import on session startup. Implement consolidation pipeline with graph algorithms (PageRank, community detection) writing pre-computed features to SQLite columns.
success_criteria: Vault export produces valid Obsidian-compatible Markdown with wikilinks and frontmatter
Batch import detects human edits via content hash comparison
Consolidation completes in <30s for 1K nodes
Pre-computed PageRank and community scores accessible as column reads at runtime

decision_points: Migration strategy: one-time JSONL→SQLite import vs gradual dual-write
Hook integration point: extend gogent-load-context or create new hook
Backward compatibility: how long to maintain JSONL fallback
implementation_phases.description: Integrate with existing memory-archivist. Implement JSONL migration path. Wire into hook chain (gogent-load-context reads from knowledge graph instead of/in addition to JSONL).
success_criteria: Existing JSONL memories successfully imported to knowledge graph
gogent-load-context retrieves memories from knowledge graph within latency budget
No regression in existing memory-archivist functionality during transition
Session handoff works with new storage backend

primary.rationale: This path captures the core value of the knowledge graph design (structured memory, vector search, Obsidian interop) while eliminating the four sources of unnecessary complexity Einstein identified. Staff-Architect's three deployment blockers are structural prerequisites that take <2 hours total to resolve. The resulting system is simpler to build, test, and maintain, with clear upgrade paths to the full architecture if needed.
primary.recommendation: Proceed with implementation using the simplified two-layer architecture (SQLite + chromem-go + sync.Map cache), IR-based scoring, write-only Obsidian vault, and consolidation-only graph algorithms. Resolve the three deployment blockers before any code is written. Update go-db-architect definition to reflect the simplified architecture before registering in agents-index.json.

supporting_evidence.from_einstein: All four simplifications rated 'high feasibility.' The cognitive science vs IR reframing is theoretically rigorous and identifies the correct optimization target (retrieval precision/recall). The consistency boundary analysis proves the three-engine design multiplies failure modes. The novel approach of pre-computing graph features during consolidation satisfies the <100ms latency budget.
supporting_evidence.from_staff_architect: APPROVE_WITH_CONDITIONS verdict confirms the agent definition is structurally sound. The seven commendations validate the research quality (sharp edges, CORRECT/WRONG patterns, dependency table, parallelization plan). The three blockers (C-1, M-1, M-3) are well-defined with exact remediation steps.

secondary.recommendation: If chromem-go's MIT license or performance proves insufficient, implement a minimal custom HNSW (~200 lines) using gonum as suggested by Staff-Architect, avoiding AGPL entirely
secondary.tradeoffs: Custom implementation requires maintenance burden and testing investment, but eliminates all external vector search dependencies and licensing concerns
secondary.when_to_use: chromem-go benchmark fails (>100ms for 100K vectors) OR MIT dual-license has unexpected restrictions

secondary.recommendation: If user demand for real-time Obsidian sync emerges post-v1, implement bidirectional sync as a v2 enhancement using the sharp edges already documented
secondary.tradeoffs: Adds the most complex subsystem in the architecture (fsnotify, debouncing, feedback loops, conflict resolution) but the sharp edges documentation already covers the pitfalls comprehensively
secondary.when_to_use: Multiple users report frustration with 'edit between sessions' model, or session durations routinely exceed 2 hours making batch-on-startup insufficient
